title: iter() basics
instruction:
`iter()` creates an iterator over references. `next()` advances it.

Run this.
successMessage: next() returns Option<&T>. Some(item) while items remain, None when exhausted. for loops call next() automatically.

title: map
instruction:
`map` transforms each element. It is lazy — nothing happens until consumed.

Run this.
successMessage: map returns a lazy iterator. collect() drives it to completion, allocating the Vec.

title: filter
instruction:
`filter` keeps elements matching a predicate.

Run this.
successMessage: filter takes a predicate closure returning bool. Only elements for which it returns true are kept.

title: fold
instruction:
`fold` accumulates a value across all elements.

Run this.
successMessage: fold starts with an accumulator, then applies the closure for each element. sum and product are common folds — Rust also provides .sum() and .product() directly.

title: enumerate
instruction:
`enumerate` pairs each element with its index.

Run this.
successMessage: enumerate yields (index, &item) tuples. More idiomatic than maintaining a counter manually.

title: zip
instruction:
`zip` combines two iterators into pairs, stopping at the shorter.

Run this.
successMessage: zip pairs elements positionally. If one iterator is shorter, the zip stops there.

title: chain
successMessage: chain concatenates lazily. copied() turns &i32 into i32 (same as map(|&x| x) but more readable).

title: Custom iterator
instruction:
Implement the `Iterator` trait for your own type by providing `next()`.

Run this.
successMessage: Implement next() and you get all iterator methods for free — sum, map, filter, collect, etc. This is the power of Rust's trait system.
